sentence: Subjective:
The patient is a 73-year-old male, Mr. Nelson, who presents for ongoing management of rheumatoid arthritis.
He reports his knee has been swollen for approximately two months, with pain level of 6 on a normal day, but currently at 3.
He denies improvement with compression socks.
He reports difficulty getting up from a seated position due to stiffness and uses a cane for support.
He has been taking Xeljanz 11 milligrams daily and Celebrex 200 milligrams daily for the past two months.
He reports his pain level improves with medication but worsens with swelling.
Past medical history: Mr. Nelson has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, for which he is being managed with Xeljanz and Celebrex.
He also reports a history of previous steroid injections in the right knee, which provided temporary relief for a couple of weeks.
Review of systems: The patient reports pain and swelling in the right knee, with associated stiffness and difficulty getting up from a seated position.
Current medications:
Xeljanz 11 milligrams daily for rheumatoid arthritis
Celebrex 200 milligrams daily for rheumatoid arthritis
Objective:
Vital signs:
Blood pressure: [NOT MENTIONED]
Pulse rate: [NOT MENTIONED]
Oxygen saturation: [NOT MENTIONED]
Temperature: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam:
Range of motion slightly diminished in the right knee
Pitting around the knee and ankle
Normal capillary refill in the toes
Knee is hot, indicating inflammation
Test Results:
[NO MENTION OF ANY LAB RESULTS OR IMAGING REPORTS]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient presents with ongoing rheumatoid arthritis, with a swollen and painful right knee, and decreased range of motion.
Problem list: Rheumatoid arthritis; Suspected fluid accumulation in the right knee; Active inflammation in the right knee.
Plan:
I prescribed Celebrex 200 milligrams daily for rheumatoid arthritis.
I administered a steroid injection to the right knee, consisting of 80 milligrams of triamcinolone and one milliliter of lidocaine.
I also ordered an x-ray of the right knee to investigate the cause of the swelling.
I recommended the patient wear compression socks and reduce salt intake to alleviate swelling.
I also advised the patient to prop their feet up, especially at night, to reduce swelling.
I instructed the patient to follow up with their primary care physician and to schedule a blood work appointment to check their Xeljanz levels.
Follow-up: The patient will follow up with me for further management and to assess the effectiveness of the treatment plan.
